exercises.title: When you actually want an encoder-decoder
exercises.prompt: Name three tasks that are naturally encoder-decoder (input→output) and explain what cross-attention does in the decoder that a pure decoder-only model handles differently.
exercises.hint: Translation, summarization, speech recognition. Cross-attention lets each decoder step attend to the fully-encoded input; decoder-only just concatenates input+output in one causal stream.
exercises.solution:
**Naturally input→output (transduction) tasks:**
- **Translation:** English → French.
- **Summarization:** 5,000-token article → 200-token summary.
- **Speech recognition:** audio/mel tokens → text tokens (e.g. Whisper).
(Also: grammar correction, text-to-SQL, transcription, style transfer.)

**What cross-attention does:** in an encoder-decoder, the encoder reads the *entire* input **bidirectionally** and produces a set of context vectors (keys/values). Each decoder block then has a **cross-attention** sublayer where the **decoder's queries attend to the encoder's keys/values** — so while generating output token by token, every step can look at the whole, fully-understood source. The decoder's *self*-attention stays causal (over the output so far); cross-attention is the bridge to the input.

**How decoder-only handles it differently:** a decoder-only model has **no separate encoder and no cross-attention**. You simply **concatenate input + output into one causal stream** ('Translate to French: <src> => <tgt>') and let causal self-attention over the prompt do the 'understanding'. It works (and is what most LLMs do), but the input is read *causally*, not bidirectionally, and there's no dedicated structure that separates 'encode the source' from 'decode the target' — it's all one sequence.

exercises.title: T5 span corruption vs BART denoising
exercises.prompt: T5 and BART are both encoder-decoder models pretrained as denoisers, but with different corruption schemes. Describe T5's span-corruption objective (with sentinel tokens) and BART's denoising objective, and explain why framing pretraining as 'corrupt → reconstruct' suits the encoder-decoder shape.
exercises.hint: T5 masks contiguous spans, replaces each with a single sentinel, decoder reproduces the dropped spans. BART corrupts (mask, delete, permute, infill) and the decoder reconstructs the ORIGINAL full text. Both are seq2seq.
exercises.solution:
**T5 — span corruption.** Pick random **contiguous spans** of tokens in the input, replace each whole span with a single unique **sentinel token** (`<X>`, `<Y>`, …). The encoder reads the corrupted text; the **decoder generates only the dropped spans**, each prefixed by its sentinel.
```text
input  : Thank you <X> me to your party <Y> week .
target : <X> for inviting <Y> last </s>
```
This is compact (the decoder only emits the missing pieces, not the whole text) and it's pure seq2seq.

**BART — denoising autoencoder.** Apply a *mix* of corruptions to the input — **token masking, token deletion, text infilling (mask a span with one [MASK]), sentence permutation, document rotation** — then the **decoder reconstructs the entire original document**. More varied noise, full-text reconstruction.

**T5's unifying twist:** T5 ('Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer') then casts **every** downstream task as text→text with a task prefix ('translate English to German: …', 'summarize: …'), so one model and one objective format handle everything.

**Why 'corrupt → reconstruct' fits encoder-decoder:** the shape is *literally* input→output — a damaged sequence in, a repaired sequence out. The **bidirectional encoder** is ideal for *understanding* the corrupted input (it can use both sides of the remaining context to infer what's missing), and the **causal decoder** is ideal for *generating* the reconstruction left-to-right. Cross-attention ties them together. Compare: BERT (encoder-only) can mask-and-predict in place but can't *generate* a variable-length output; GPT (decoder-only) generates but reads input causally. Denoising naturally exercises both an understanding module and a generation module — exactly what enc-dec provides — which is why T5/BART pretraining is built this way.

exercises.title: Why decoder-only displaced encoder-decoder for general LLMs
exercises.prompt: Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) were SOTA for seq2seq, yet general-purpose 2026 LLMs are overwhelmingly decoder-only. Explain the tradeoffs: where encoder-decoder still genuinely wins, why decoder-only scaled better as a single general model, and what 'prefix LM' / shared-trunk ideas borrow from both.
exercises.hint: Enc-dec: bidirectional input encoding, parameter split, strong on fixed transduction (Whisper). Decoder-only: one stack, task unification, KV-cache serving, in-context learning. Prefix-LM: bidirectional attention over the prompt, causal over the completion in one stack.
exercises.solution:
**Where encoder-decoder genuinely still wins:**
- **Fixed transduction with a clear, bounded input** — translation, summarization, and especially **speech recognition (Whisper is encoder-decoder)** and other modality→text tasks. The **bidirectional encoder** gives a richer representation of the *whole* input than a causal pass, and cross-attention cleanly separates 'understand source' from 'produce target'.
- Tasks where input and output are very different modalities/lengths and you benefit from a dedicated encoder.

**Why decoder-only scaled better as one general model:**
1. **Task unification:** everything is 'completion', so a single causal LM + prompting/instruction-tuning subsumes translation, summarization, QA, chat, code — no architectural switch per task.
2. **Simplicity at scale:** one stack, one objective (next-token), dense self-supervised signal on raw text. Fewer components to tune than enc-dec; easier to scale parameters and data.
3. **Serving efficiency:** decoder-only pairs perfectly with the **KV cache** — prefill the prompt, then cheap incremental decode. Enc-dec adds an encoder pass and cross-attention KV management.
4. **In-context / few-shot learning** emerges strongly in large decoder-only models, which enc-dec didn't match for open-ended use.
5. **Data efficiency of the objective:** next-token gives a label at *every* position over unlimited text; denoising needs a corruption scheme and reconstructs fewer effective targets.

**Hybrid / borrowed ideas:**
- **Prefix-LM (UL2, some T5 variants):** a single stack that uses **bidirectional attention over the prompt/prefix** (like an encoder) and **causal attention over the completion** (like a decoder) — capturing enc-dec's bidirectional input understanding without a second tower. This is a practical middle ground and informs how some models treat the prompt vs the generation.
- **UL2's mixture-of-denoisers** blends span-corruption and causal objectives in one model.
- Multimodal LLMs effectively keep an **encoder for the non-text modality** (vision/audio encoder) feeding a **decoder-only LLM** — i.e. they resurrect the enc-dec split exactly where bidirectional encoding of a fixed input still pays off, while keeping the unified decoder for generation.

**Bottom line:** decoder-only won the *general assistant* race on unification, scaling simplicity, and serving; encoder-decoder remains the right tool for **fixed input→output transduction**, and the strongest systems quietly combine both (encode the hard input modality, decode with a unified causal LM).
